---
title: "Geocomputation with R"
subtitle: "Introduction"
author: "Jannes Muenchow, Jakub Nowosad"
date: 2026-07-06
---

## Who we are

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="62%"}
**Welcome** to our tutorial!

We are the authors of
[Geocomputation with R](https://r.geocompx.org/) (2nd edition).

- [Robin Lovelace](https://www.robinlovelace.net/) — Professor of Transport Data
  Science, Leeds; open-source tools for sustainable transport ([pct.bike](https://www.pct.bike/)).

- [Jakub Nowosad](https://jakubnowosad.com/) — Associate Professor, Poznań;
  spatial machine learning; creator of **landscapemetrics** and **supercells**.

- [Jannes Muenchow](https://cynkra.com/) — Senior Data Scientist & DevOps Engineer at [cynkra](https://cynkra.com/); R-GIS integration, e.g., co-author of **qgisprocess**.
:::

::: {.column width="38%"}
![](/img/cover2.png){width="80%"}
:::
:::

## Slides, code & Docker image

Everything for today is online:

- **Slides & code:** <https://github.com/geocompx/user26>
- **The book (free to read):** <https://r.geocompx.org/>

. . .

A **Docker image** with R, RStudio and all packages pre-installed:

```sh
docker run -e DISABLE_AUTH=true --rm -p 8787:8787 \
  ghcr.io/geocompx/latest
```

Then open <http://localhost:8787> (log in `rstudio` / `pw`).
On Apple Silicon add `--platform linux/amd64`.

. . .

**Docker is optional.** For most exercises any IDE (RStudio / Positron /
VS Code) with R and a few packages is enough:

```r
install.packages(c("sf", "dplyr", "terra", "tmap", "spData"))
```

More images, incl. the full QGIS stack: <https://github.com/geocompx/docker>.

## Where we are from

```{r}
#| echo: false
library(leaflet)
popup <- c("Robin (Leeds)", "Jakub (Poznań)", "Jannes (Erlangen)")
leaflet() |>
  addProviderTiles("CartoDB.Positron") |>
  addMarkers(
    lng = c(-1.5, 16.9, 11.01),
    lat = c(53.8, 52.4, 49.6),
    popup = popup
  )
```

## About the book

The online home of the book: <https://r.geocompx.org/>

Source code: <https://github.com/geocompx/geocompr>

. . .

Part of the **geocompx** project — open, reproducible geocomputation resources
for both **R** and **Python**: <https://geocompx.org/>

. . .

The book is free to read online and covers everything in this tutorial in much
more depth.

## Contents of the tutorial

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
::: {.incremental}
1. Intro - definitions and brief history of spatial R
1. Coordinate reference systems
1. Spatial **vector** data with `sf` [@pebesma_simple_2018]
1. Spatial **raster** data with `terra` [@r-terra]
1. **Mapping** / visualization with `tmap` [@tennekes_tmap_2018]
1. *(time permitting)* R GIS bridges with `qgisprocess` [@r-qgisprocess]
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
![](/img/globe.png)
:::
:::

# Some definitions {.section-divider}

## What is a GIS?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
::: {.incremental}
- A **G**eographic **I**nformation **S**ystem is a system for the analysis,
  manipulation and visualization of geographic data [@longley_geographic_2015].
- Six components of a GIS: software, data, procedures, hardware, people, network.
- Typical GIS software: QGIS, SAGA-GIS, GRASS-GIS, ArcGIS Pro (commercial).
:::
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
![](/img/gis_logos.png)
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
R was never intended to be a GIS, though it is great that we can use it as one.
R has been designed from the beginning as an interface to other languages — later
we can build bridges from R to GIS software.

On the six components (Longley et al.): **procedures** = the methods/workflows for
operating the GIS (broader than just spatial algorithms); **network** =
connectivity / the Internet for sharing data and web GIS — *not* a spatial
road/river network.
:::

## What is geocomputation?

- Geocomputation
- GIScience
- Geographic data science

. . .

> GeoComputation is about ... developing relevant geo-tools within the overall
> context of a "scientific" approach [@openshaw_geocomputation_2000].

. . .

About harnessing the power of modern computers to **do things** with geographic data.

## GUI GIS vs geocomputation with R

| Attribute            | Desktop GIS (GUI)        | R                          |
|----------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| Emphasis             | visual interaction       | code / reproducibility     |
| Reproducibility      | minimal                  | maximal                    |
| Learning curve       | shallow                  | steep(er)                  |
| Automation / scaling | difficult                | straightforward            |

: {tbl-colwidths="[28,36,36]"}

Further reading:
<https://r.geocompx.org/intro.html#what-is-geocomputation>

## Geographic data models

- Geographic data consist of **coordinates** and corresponding **attributes**
  (possibly with a time stamp).

. . .

- Geographic data are only a **simplified representation** of the world — we
  decide what to represent, and for which purpose.

. . .

- Geographic data can quickly become **big**.

. . .

- Two data models for representing geographic data digitally: the **vector**
  [@pebesma_simple_2018] and the **raster** [@r-terra] data model.

# Vector and raster {.section-divider}

## Vector vs raster

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Vector**

![](/img/vector.png){width="80%"}

Points, lines, polygons — discrete objects with well-defined borders.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Raster**

![](/img/raster.png){width="80%"}

A grid of cells — continuous fields (elevation, temperature, imagery).
:::
:::

# A brief history of spatial data in R {.section-divider}

## Geographic analysis in ... S

```{r}
#| include: false
if (!requireNamespace("splancs", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("splancs", repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
}
```

**splancs** [@rowlingson_splancs_1993] — point-pattern analysis code written for
**S**, R's predecessor. 30 years on, it **still works just fine.**

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%" .fragment}

<br>
```{r}
#| eval: false
R.version.string
library(splancs)
data(bodmin)
polymap(bodmin$poly)
pointmap(
  as.points(bodmin),
  add = TRUE,
  pch = 16,
  col = "#2B66AB"
)
```
:::

::: {.column width="52%" .fragment}
<br>
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 4.2
R.version.string
library(splancs)
data(bodmin)
polymap(bodmin$poly)
pointmap(
  as.points(bodmin),
  add = TRUE,
  pch = 16,
  col = "#2B66AB"
)
```
:::
:::

## The classic R-spatial stack

The 2000s: a coherent set of packages established R as a serious spatial tool.

- **sp** (2005) — spatial classes (`SpatialPointsDataFrame`, ..., @bivand_applied_2013).
- **rgdal** — bindings to GDAL/PROJ for I/O and reprojection [@r-rgdal].
- **rgeos** — bindings to GEOS for geometry operations [@r-rgeos].
- **raster** (2010) — the raster data model [@r-raster].

. . .

Documented in *Applied Spatial Data Analysis with R* [@bivand_applied_2013].

## The modern R-spatial stack

- **sf** (2016) implements the **simple features** standard, integrates with the
  tidyverse, and links directly to GDAL / GEOS / PROJ [@pebesma_simple_2018].
- **terra** replaces `raster` — faster, C++-based, larger-than-memory support
  [@r-terra]
- **stars** for spatiotemporal data cubes.

. . .

Today, `sf` + `terra` are the standard — see also *Spatial Data Science* [@pebesma_spatial_2023].
In **2023**, the classic `maptools` / `rgdal` / `rgeos` / `rgrass7` packages were **retired**.

## It all rests on GDAL, GEOS and PROJ

::::: {.gdal-stack}

:::: {.stack-top}
**sf** · **terra** — R packages
::::

:::: {.stack-libs}

::: {.lib-box}
**GDAL**

[read / write data formats]{.lib-sub}
:::

::: {.lib-box}
**GEOS**

[geometry operations]{.lib-sub}
:::

::: {.lib-box}
**PROJ**

[coordinate reference systems]{.lib-sub}
:::

::::

:::::

The same C/C++ libraries power **QGIS**, **GRASS** and much of the geospatial
world.

## Why R? (and does it really matter?)

- **Reproducible research** — from raw data to statistics to maps to report,
  all in one scripted workflow (Quarto, `renv`).

. . .

- A **rich spatial ecosystem** — hundreds of packages: `sf`, `terra`, `tmap`,
  `spatstat`, `gstat`, ...

. . .

- A **great plotting engine** — `ggplot2` and `tmap` make publication-quality maps.

. . .

- Could you do this in another scripted language? **Sure** — the concepts
  transfer, and R and Python share the same engines (GDAL / GEOS / PROJ) and
  formats. But R does it well, so: **why not R.**

## Let's get started

Next up: **coordinate reference systems**.

Slides and code: <https://github.com/geocompx/user26>

## References {.smaller}

::: {#refs}
:::
